takeaways: For planners and EPCs: include storage in project designs to manage renewable variability and meet availability targets.
For utilities and grid operators: ESS offer dispatchable flexibility and ancillary services that aid integration of renewables.
For data center and critical-infrastructure teams: consider storage solutions to improve resilience and ensure continuous power from renewable sources.

It likely considers technical and operational approaches for shifting or modulating data center demand to relieve grid constraints and support integration of large AI power draws.
bullets: Focuses on data centers and AI as rapidly growing, high-density electricity consumers.
Explores the concept of load flexibility as a mechanism to unlock grid capacity.

takeaways: If data center/AI load flexibility can be operationalized, it could reduce near-term need for new transmission or substation upgrades by smoothing peak demand.
Successful implementation will require real‑time controls, telemetry, and contractual mechanisms between data centers, utilities, and aggregators.
Interconnection studies and planning processes may need new methodologies to value flexible, time‑shifted demand versus traditional firm capacity.
EPCs and grid planners should evaluate retrofit and design options that enable demand-side flexibility (e.g., on-site storage, workload scheduling interfaces).
